ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent; no specific vegetable broth ramen code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of vegetable broth ramen weighing approximately 450 g, comprising ramen noodles in a clear vegetable broth with toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle soup, vegetable broth — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for vegetable broth ramen exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for: (1) cooked ramen noodles (FDC ID 168936), (2) vegetable broth/stock (FDC ID 171158), and (3) mixed vegetable toppings, combined at approximate proportions of 40% noodles, 50% broth, 10% vegetables by weight. Per-100g values reflect this composite. Sodium is elevated due to soy-sauce-seasoned broth typical of ramen preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; ramen noodles (wheat-based, boiled) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetable broth ramen as a composite dish. GI of 52 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat-based ramen noodles (boiled), which is the dominant glycemic contributor in this dish. The broth and vegetable components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 44.1) / 100 ≈ 23.0, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 9.8 × 4.5 = 44.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-sauce-seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g bowl of vegetable broth ramen provides approximately 1440 mg sodium, representing 60–72% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size significantly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat-based ramen noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 and a glycemic load of ~23 per bowl, vegetable broth ramen has a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, consider lower-GI noodle substitutes (e.g., konjac or soba), and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate carbohydrate load; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Vegetable broth ramen is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, which is favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the high sodium content from the seasoned broth poses a risk for blood pressure management in individuals with cardiovascular disease, and portion control or sodium reduction is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Broth Ramen is a Japanese-style noodle soup dish consisting of springy wheat-based ramen noodles served in a clear, slow-simmered vegetable broth. The broth is typically prepared by extracting flavours from kombu (dried kelp), shiitake mushrooms, carrots, onions, garlic, and ginger over an extended cooking period, then seasoned with soy sauce and mirin to achieve a savoury umami depth. The dish is vegetarian and vegan-friendly, making it suitable for plant-based diners. In Singapore, it is offered as a lighter, health-conscious alternative to pork- or chicken-based ramen broths. Texturally, the noodles are chewy and the broth is light yet flavourful. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in calories (~234 kcal per bowl), low in fat, and provides a moderate amount of carbohydrate from the noodles. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to the soy-sauce-seasoned broth; a single bowl can contribute over 1400 mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 52 (low-moderate), driven by the wheat noodles. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans, and is halal-friendly. Clinically, it is advisable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease to moderate intake due to sodium content, while those with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and noodle quantity.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for vegetable broth ramen exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or a primary regional database. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite of cooked ramen noodles, vegetable broth, and mixed vegetables at representative proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for wheat-based boiled ramen noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked ramen noodles (FDC 168936), vegetable broth (FDC 171158), and mixed vegetables at approximate proportions (40% noodles, 50% broth, 10% vegetables by weight). Direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish from Flavours by Saute - Funan is not available. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0; confirmed zero as no vitamin-D-fortified ingredients are present and plant-based broths do not naturally contain vitamin D. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as the dish is entirely plant-based. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are used. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 0.1 estimated from composite). 7. GI set to 52 based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat ramen noodles; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 8. Sodium content is notably high (~320 mg/100g; ~1440 mg per 450g bowl) due to soy-sauce-seasoned broth; this is the primary clinical concern. 9. The food is served at Flavours by Saute - Funan, Singapore, via field survey. 10. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Japanese, reflecting both the Japanese cuisine origin and the Singapore serving context. Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese local_names are null as this dish has no established native names in those languages. 11. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from the USDA composite; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
